proposals.title: Quantifying the Amazon's Drought-Induced Carbon Loss: A Satellite-Based Water Deficit Scaling Framework
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Malhi's work on increasing tree size and compounding human influence, shows Amazonian forests are critical carbon sinks under threat. Intensifying droughts, like those of 2005, 2010, and 2015, cause significant tree mortality and carbon loss, but quantifying the basin-wide carbon flux impact of these events remains a major uncertainty in global carbon cycle models. A scalable, observation-based method is urgently needed to move from localized forest plot data to continental-scale carbon budget assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robust, non-linear relationship exists between satellite-derived water deficit (a measure of drought intensity and duration) and plot-observed carbon loss, and that this relationship can be applied across the Amazon basin to accurately estimate regional carbon flux anomalies during major drought events.
proposals.method: We will first establish the empirical relationship using pre-2026 data from the RAINFOR and GEM networks, linking plot-level carbon balance to a high-resolution water deficit index (WDI) derived from MODIS land surface temperature and TRMM/GPM precipitation data. This site-level model will then be applied continentally using wall-to-wall WDI maps from MODIS and SMOS/ SMAP soil moisture. The scaling will employ a machine learning regression framework (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) trained on the plot data and spatially explicit covariates (forest type, biomass, soils) to predict grid-cell carbon loss, which will be aggregated to basin-wide totals.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first observationally constrained, basin-wide estimate of drought-driven carbon loss for major Amazonian droughts, directly informing IPCC-class vegetation models. It will provide a scalable monitoring framework for near-real-time assessment of carbon flux anomalies during future droughts, bridging the critical gap between intensive forest science and satellite remote sensing.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate drought with greenness or inferred stress, this proposal novelly scales a direct, ground-truthed carbon loss response to continental scales using a physically coherent water deficit metric, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic, quantifiable flux. It integrates Malhi's deep plot-based understanding of forest dynamics with emerging satellite hydrology products in a novel data fusion approach.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Amazonian Drought History by Inverting Forest Structure and Biomass Patterns
proposals.motivation: Amazonian forests are facing increasing drought frequency and intensity, but instrumental and paleo-records of drought are spatially and temporally limited. This hampers our understanding of long-term forest resilience. A method to infer past drought regimes from the forest's current state would provide a novel, spatially extensive climate record and reveal how historical stress has shaped contemporary ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of forest structural traits (e.g., tree size distribution, wood density, above-ground biomass) and species composition encodes a quantifiable signature of past drought severity and frequency, which can be statistically inverted to reconstruct historical hydroclimate.
proposals.method: We will compile a large pre-2026 dataset of forest inventory plots across Amazonia, pairing plot-level metrics (basal area, stem density, size class distribution, community-weighted mean wood density) with long-term local climate data. Using a Bayesian hierarchical model, we will establish the forward relationship between drought indices (e.g., SPEI) and forest structural response. We will then apply an inverse modelling approach (Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling) to this calibrated model, using forest structure from independent plots to infer the most likely historical drought regime that produced the observed biomass and community patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, forest-derived map of historical drought stress for the Amazon, complementing existing climate reconstructions. It will directly test the long-term legacies of drought on carbon stocks and biodiversity, informing models of forest resilience under future climate change.
proposals.novelty: While previous work links current drought to forest dynamics, this inverts the problem, using the forest as an archive. It moves beyond correlative studies to a formal inverse framework, integrating demography and functional traits to decode climate history from standing biomass.

proposals.title: Integrating Forest Demography and Ecosystem Function to Prioritize Climate-Resilient Management in Tropical Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Tropical forests are critical for global climate regulation, yet they face compounding threats from climate change and human disturbance. Current management often lacks a mechanistic link between on-the-ground forest dynamics (e.g., increasing tree size, compositional shifts) and their consequences for ecosystem benefits like carbon storage and biodiversity. A research priority framework is urgently needed to translate scientific insights into actionable strategies for conservation and restoration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating demographic metrics of forest change (e.g., size-structure shifts, mortality/growth rates) with functional trait and carbon density data will reveal key leverage points where management intervention can most effectively enhance ecosystem resilience and climate mitigation benefits.
proposals.method: We will synthesize long-term forest plot data (e.g., RAINFOR, ForestPlots.net) across Amazonia to quantify demographic trends. This will be integrated with airborne LiDAR-derived canopy structure and carbon density maps. Using structural equation modeling (SEM) and gradient boosting machine learning (e.g., XGBoost), we will identify the dominant drivers (climate, soil, disturbance history) of demographic and functional change. Management priority zones will be mapped by overlaying these model outputs with socio-economic layers of human influence and protected area status.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a spatially explicit, evidence-based framework for prioritizing research and management actions in tropical landscapes. It will directly inform where interventions like assisted natural regeneration, protection, or enrichment planting could maximize co-benefits for carbon, biodiversity, and ecosystem services, moving beyond generic policy targets.
proposals.novelty: While forest demography and carbon science are advanced, their explicit integration into a practical, spatially-prioritized management framework is novel. This approach moves from documenting change to diagnosing its causes and identifying actionable intervention points, bridging a critical gap between academic understanding and implementation.

proposals.title: Satellite-derived early-warning signals of critical slowing down in the Amazon and boreal forest biomes
proposals.motivation: Major forest biomes like the Amazon and boreal forests are under increasing pressure from climate change and land-use, with growing evidence they may approach tipping points leading to large-scale, irreversible degradation. Pre-2026, early-warning signals (EWS) based on 'critical slowing down' have been theorized and tested in models and limited field data, but a scalable, observation-based methodology for global biomes is lacking. This gap hinders proactive policy and conservation efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: That pre-tipping point 'critical slowing down' in major forest biomes can be detected from multi-decadal satellite time-series as increased temporal autocorrelation and variance in key ecosystem state variables, and that these signals will be spatially heterogeneous, correlating with known gradients of forest resilience.
proposals.method: We will use the Landsat archive (1984-2025) and MODIS data (2000-2025) to compute annual biome-scale metrics of forest state: canopy moisture (NDMI), greenness (EVI), and surface temperature. For each pixel time-series, we will apply rolling-window statistical algorithms to calculate Kendall's τ for lag-1 autocorrelation (AR1) and variance. We will test for significant increasing trends in these EWS metrics, mapping their spatial distribution. Analysis will be stratified by regions of known stress (e.g., Amazonian dry season length, boreal warming trends) and forest properties (e.g., tree size/density from Malhi's prior work).
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first spatially explicit, observation-based map of potential EWS for the world's two largest forest biomes. It will directly test tipping point theory at a continental scale, moving beyond models. The work will provide a concrete methodological framework for operational biome resilience monitoring using freely available satellite data.
proposals.novelty: While EWS theory is established, its application to real-world, satellite-derived vegetation data at a biome scale is novel. The integration of long-term forest change context (e.g., increasing tree size, compounding human influence) with statistical physics-based tipping point indicators represents a significant advance over traditional trend analysis.

proposals.title: Inverse Ecosystem Design: Quantifying the Amazonian Forest Structure and Composition Required to Stabilize Regional Climate Under Future Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Climate stabilization relies on ecosystem feedbacks, but current models primarily project ecosystem responses to climate, not the reverse. Given the critical role of the Amazon in global carbon and water cycles, and evidence of increasing tree size and compounding human influence, we urgently need to define the forest states that can actively maintain regional climate resilience. This shifts the paradigm from passive prediction to active design for climate services.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given future climate scenario, there exists a quantifiable, optimal combination of forest structure (e.g., biomass density, canopy height) and species trait composition (e.g., drought tolerance, albedo) that maximizes the probability of stabilizing local and regional climate through biophysical and biogeochemical feedbacks.
proposals.method: We will couple a trait- and size-structured forest demographic model (e.g., TROLL or a modified ED2) with a regional climate model (WRF) in a novel inverse modeling framework. Using Bayesian optimization and genetic algorithms, we will iteratively adjust initial forest parameters (species pools, size distributions, functional trait values) within the coupled model to minimize the divergence of key climate variables (precipitation, temperature) from a defined 'stable' target state under future forcing scenarios (e.g., RCP 4.5). The analysis will focus on the Amazon, leveraging pre-2026 forest inventory and remote sensing data (e.g., GEDI, RAINFOR) for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit 'blueprints' of forest states targeted for climate stabilization, moving beyond carbon-centric restoration goals. It will quantify the trade-offs between forest composition for carbon sequestration versus biophysical cooling. The framework will provide a novel science-based tool for guiding large-scale ecosystem management and restoration aimed at climate mitigation.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of climate-ecosystem interactions is mature, inverting the problem to design ecosystems for a climate target is a fundamental conceptual shift. The novelty lies in the specific integration of demographic forest models with climate models in an inverse optimization framework to solve for ecosystem parameters, rather than projecting their fate.

proposals.title: Dynamic Trait-Based Modelling of Amazonian Forest Composition and Carbon Under Climate Change
proposals.motivation: Current Earth System Models (ESMs) and Dynamic Global Vegetation Models (DGVMs) often rely on static maps of potential vegetation types or coarse plant functional types, limiting their ability to project realistic biome shifts and ecosystem feedbacks. This is a critical gap for the Amazon, where pre-2026 research by Malhi and others shows increasing tree size and compounding human influence, making the forest's future carbon storage highly uncertain under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modelling Amazonian vegetation as a dynamic assemblage of species-level functional traits, constrained by observed allometry and demography, will project fundamentally different and more spatially heterogeneous shifts in forest composition and carbon stocks under future climates than models using static vegetation classes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel DGVM module that simulates forest communities based on key functional traits (e.g., wood density, maximum height, drought tolerance) drawn from pre-2026 databases like TRY. The module will use a trait-filtering algorithm, where environmental gradients (temperature, water deficit, CO2) probabilistically filter species pools at each grid cell. Forest dynamics will be simulated using an individual-based, size-structured demographic approach, parameterized with Amazon-wide data on tree growth, mortality, and recruitment from Malhi's prior work. Climate forcing will use CMIP6 scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more mechanistic, spatially explicit projection of Amazon forest transition zones and carbon vulnerability, directly informing conservation priorities. It will advance vegetation modelling by bridging the gap between species-level ecology and global-scale biogeochemical forecasting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating observed, hyper-local trait variation and allometric scaling from permanent forest plots into a DGVM framework, moving beyond static biome maps to simulate emergent vegetation types as dynamic outcomes of climate-driven trait filtering.

proposals.title: Biome-specific carbon-nitrogen coupling modulates global ecosystem resilience to El Niño-Southern Oscillation events
proposals.motivation: El Niño events are major climate perturbations that drive global-scale shifts in carbon cycling and forest dynamics, but their impacts are highly heterogeneous across biomes. Pre-2026 research, including Malhi's work on increasing tree size and compounding human influence in Amazonia, highlights that nutrient availability, particularly nitrogen, may critically mediate ecosystem carbon balance during climatic stress. A systematic, biome-wide assessment of Carbon-Nitrogen (C-N) interactions during El Niño is lacking but essential for predicting future climate-biosphere feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and sign of C-N interaction effects on ecosystem carbon balance during El Niño events are biome-specific, driven by baseline nutrient status and community traits, with nitrogen limitation amplifying carbon loss in temperate and boreal forests but potentially mitigating loss in some tropical forests.
proposals.method: We will extend a factorial modeling experiment (e.g., within the Community Land Model, CLM5, or JULES) to all major terrestrial biomes. The experiment will force models with historical El Niño climate anomalies (e.g., 1997-98, 2015-16) under two factorial conditions: with dynamic C-N coupling enabled and disabled. We will analyze biome-specific differences in the response of key carbon fluxes (NPP, heterotrophic respiration) and stocks to isolate the C-N interaction effect. The analysis will leverage pre-2026 remote sensing data (e.g., solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence) and forest inventory networks for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global map of nutrient-mediated vulnerability to El Niño, quantifying where nitrogen cycling most strongly buffers or exacerbates carbon cycle perturbations. It will directly inform Earth System Model development by identifying biomes where improved C-N cycle representation is most critical for climate projections. The findings will also guide conservation and management by highlighting regions where nutrient status influences climate resilience.
proposals.novelty: While C-N dynamics have been studied in isolation, applying a consistent factorial experimental framework across all biomes to dissect their role during a specific, major climate mode is novel. This moves beyond documenting El Niño impacts to mechanistically explaining biome-level differences in response, integrating Malhi's expertise in tropical forest carbon science with a global biogeochemical perspective.

proposals.title: A complete pan-tropical carbon budget: integrating wetlands and agricultural lands into the Terrestrial Ecosystem Model (TEM) framework
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale carbon budget models, including TEM, often exclude or poorly represent wetland and agricultural vegetation types, focusing primarily on natural, non-wetland forests. This creates a critical gap in our understanding of the full continental carbon cycle, as wetlands are significant carbon sinks and sources, and managed lands dominate vast tropical landscapes. A complete budget is essential for accurate climate mitigation policy and understanding the net carbon benefits of tropical landscapes under human influence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating wetland biogeochemistry and the dynamics of major crop and pasture systems into TEM will reveal a significantly different, and more variable, net continental carbon balance for the tropics, with managed lands acting as a substantial net carbon source partially offset by wetland sequestration.
proposals.method: We will expand the TEM framework by integrating two new modules: 1) a wetland module incorporating pre-2026 algorithms for anaerobic soil decomposition, methane production/oxidation, and specific plant functional types (e.g., palms, herbaceous vegetation) calibrated with field data from pantropical flux towers and soil cores. 2) An agricultural module parameterizing key crops (e.g., oil palm, soybean, pasture grasses) using management regimes (fertilization, harvest) derived from FAO and remote-sensing data. Model runs will be forced with high-resolution climate and land-use change data (e.g., MapBiomas, TerraClimate) from 2000-2025 across the Amazon, Congo, and Southeast Asia.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pantropical carbon budget that explicitly and mechanistically includes wetlands and agriculture, quantifying their relative contributions to the net land-atmosphere flux. It will directly inform IPCC assessments on land-use change emissions and natural climate solutions, providing a more robust basis for policies targeting carbon sequestration in human-modified tropical landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have modeled wetlands or agriculture in isolation, this proposal integrates them into a unified, process-based ecosystem model (TEM) used for continental-scale forest carbon assessment, enabling a direct comparison of fluxes across all major tropical land cover types within a single analytical framework.

proposals.title: Divergent Trajectories: Projecting the Future Carbon Balance of North American Forests Under Climate Change Using a Calibrated Terrestrial Ecosystem Model
proposals.motivation: North American forests are a critical global carbon sink, but their future stability is uncertain under climate change. Pre-2026 evidence from Amazonian and other tropical forests shows complex, compounding human and climatic influences on tree growth and mortality, suggesting similar dynamics may unfold in temperate and boreal systems. Quantifying the net effect of these opposing processes—enhanced growth versus increased stress—is essential for accurate global carbon budgeting and climate policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that climate change will drive a divergence in the carbon balance of North American forests, with initial increases in Net Primary Productivity (NPP) in northern regions being offset or surpassed by long-term declines in carbon storage due to increasing tree mortality and compositional shifts.
proposals.method: We will use a calibrated Terrestrial Ecosystem Model (TEM) framework, informed by pre-2026 forest inventory data (e.g., tree size, density) and flux tower measurements. The model will be driven by downscaled climate scenario data (CMIP6; SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) to project changes in NPP and carbon storage to 2100. Key innovations include integrating observed relationships between tree size, mortality risk, and carbon density, and implementing region-specific parameterizations for drought and heat stress derived from recent tropical forest studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a spatially explicit, process-based projection of the North American forest carbon sink, directly informing continental-scale climate mitigation strategies. It will advance understanding of how the benefits of carbon sequestration by trees and nature are likely to change, offering a critical test of whether observed increases in tree size can compensate for rising ecosystem stress.
proposals.novelty: While TEMs are widely used, this proposal's novelty lies in its explicit calibration using recent empirical insights on tree demography and mortality drivers from tropical systems, applied to forecast carbon balance in a temperate/boreal context under novel future climates.

proposals.title: Stand-scale carbon and nitrogen dynamics in Amazonian forests: A mechanistic downscaling of the TEM framework using individual-based canopy and soil process models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale ecosystem models like the Terrestrial Ecosystem Model (TEM) are crucial for projecting regional carbon budgets but often fail to capture the fine-scale heterogeneity in forest structure and function that underpins resilience and biodiversity benefits. Recent findings of increasing tree size and compounding human influence in Amazonia highlight the need to understand how stand-scale dynamics, driven by individual tree competition and soil resource gradients, mediate ecosystem responses to global change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that downscaling the TEM C-N framework to the stand level, incorporating individual tree canopy processes and detailed soil biogeochemistry, will reveal that the net primary productivity and carbon storage of Amazonian forests are more sensitive to shifts in species composition and canopy structure than predicted by area-averaged models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the TEM C-N cycling algorithms to a 1-ha stand scale for well-studied Amazonian forest plots. Canopy processes will be simulated using an individual-based, height-structured light competition model (e.g., incorporating allometric scaling from Malhi's tree size work). Soil processes will be modeled with a multi-pool soil organic matter model resolving depth-dependent decomposition. The coupled model will be validated against long-term plot census data, eddy covariance fluxes, and soil respiration measurements, using Bayesian calibration to constrain parameters related to individual tree energetics and soil carbon density.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistic bridge between individual tree ecology and regional biogeochemistry, directly testing how community traits influence stand-level carbon sequestration. It will deliver a validated modeling framework to assess the carbon consequences of changing forest structure and composition under future climates, directly informing conservation and forest management strategies.
proposals.novelty: While TEM has been applied regionally, its explicit downscaling to a mechanistic, individual-based stand model that integrates canopy architecture with soil process validation represents a significant advance beyond phenomenological stand models, directly linking Malhi's work on tree size and community change to ecosystem function.

proposals.title: A global terrestrial ecosystem model (TEM) framework: scaling biome-specific carbon dynamics from Amazonian insights to a pan-biome carbon balance
proposals.motivation: Accurately quantifying the global terrestrial carbon sink is critical for predicting future climate trajectories and informing climate policy. Pre-2026, large uncertainties persist in biome-specific contributions, particularly in tropical forests where processes like increasing tree size and compounding human influence, as documented by Malhi's work, are not fully captured in global models. Extending a robust, calibrated model framework like TEM, which has proven effective in North America, to all major biomes would provide a more mechanistic and consistent global estimate.
proposals.hypothesis: That the core physiological and biogeochemical processes in the TEM framework, when parameterized with biome-specific data on forest structure, demography, and disturbance, can accurately simulate the contemporary carbon balance across the world's major terrestrial biomes, reducing uncertainty in the global sink.
proposals.method: We will implement a global TEM framework by first adapting its core algorithms for photosynthesis, respiration, and carbon allocation. Biome-specific parameterization will use pre-2026 remote sensing (e.g., MODIS, GEDI) for canopy structure and forest density, and harmonized global soil and climate datasets. Key innovations include integrating Malhi's empirical findings on Amazonian tree size distributions and mortality regimes as biome-specific modules, and implementing a nested scaling approach where plot-level demographic data constrains grid-cell simulations. Model calibration and validation will leverage global carbon flux tower networks (FLUXNET) and biomass inventory data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first globally consistent, biome-resolved carbon flux and stock estimates from a TEM-class model, directly quantifying the contribution of different biomes to the global sink. It will provide a novel tool to attribute changes in the carbon balance to specific drivers (climate, CO2, land use) across biomes, directly informing the Global Carbon Project and IPCC assessments.
proposals.novelty: While TEM has been applied regionally, a global implementation that explicitly incorporates recent tropical forest science on tree demography and human influence represents a significant advance over static biome maps in Earth System Models, moving towards a dynamic global vegetation model grounded in empirical tree-level processes.

proposals.title: Coupling Nitrogen Limitation and Climate Feedbacks in Tropical Forests: A TEM-ESM Integration to Quantify Impacts on Surface Energy and Water Fluxes
proposals.motivation: Tropical forests are critical for global carbon and water cycles, yet Earth System Models (ESMs) largely assume plant productivity is limited by climate and CO2 alone, neglecting widespread nutrient constraints. Pre-2026 evidence, including increasing tree size but potentially constrained growth in Amazonia, suggests nitrogen (N) limitation may significantly modulate Net Primary Productivity (NPP). This omission creates major uncertainty in predicting forest-climate feedbacks, as N-limited NPP directly influences surface energy partitioning (latent vs. sensible heat) and water fluxes through transpiration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating dynamic nitrogen limitation into a coupled vegetation-climate model will reveal a significant dampening of projected increases in tropical forest carbon sequestration and transpiration, thereby altering regional climate projections through modified surface energy and water fluxes.
proposals.method: We will couple the Terrestrial Ecosystem Model (TEM), which includes a well-tested N cycling sub-model, to the land surface component of a climate model such as the Community Earth System Model (CESM). This involves implementing TEM's prognostic N cycling algorithms (e.g., N mineralization, plant uptake, leaching) within the Community Land Model (CLM) framework to dynamically constrain NPP. We will conduct twin simulations for the Amazon basin (historical and future RCP scenarios): one with C-only limitation and one with C-N co-limitation. The analysis will focus on differences in key output variables: NPP, leaf area index, latent heat flux, surface temperature, and runoff.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, basin-scale assessment of how nitrogen limitation modulates climate-vegetation feedbacks in the tropics. It will deliver improved constraints on the future carbon sink potential of Amazonian forests and produce novel insights into how nutrient cycling can regionally influence the hydrological cycle and surface climate.
proposals.novelty: While N limitation is studied in biogeochemistry, its explicit two-way coupling with a full climate model to assess impacts on surface energy and water budgets in tropical forests is a novel integration. This moves beyond offline impact studies to capture critical feedbacks where altered transpiration influences local climate, which in turn affects NPP.

proposals.title: Coupling Carbon and Nitrogen Cycles in the Urban Biome: A Hyper-Resolution Model for Green Infrastructure Productivity and Climate Benefits
proposals.motivation: Urban forests and green infrastructure are critical for climate mitigation and adaptation, yet their productivity and carbon sequestration potential are poorly quantified at city scales. Pre-2026 ecosystem models largely ignore urban biomes or treat them simplistically, neglecting key constraints like nitrogen availability from pollution and fertilization, which strongly couple to carbon cycling in natural forests. A mechanistic, fine-scale model is needed to predict the real benefits of urban nature for carbon storage and human well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating coupled carbon-nitrogen cycle dynamics into a hyper-resolution urban land surface model will reveal that nitrogen deposition and management are primary drivers of spatial heterogeneity in urban forest productivity and carbon sequestration, substantially modifying estimates based on carbon-only or coarse-scale approaches.
proposals.method: We will adapt and couple the nitrogen cycle module from the Community Land Model (CLM) or the JULES model into a hyper-resolution (1-10m grid) urban ecosystem model framework. The model will be parameterized using pre-2026 data on urban tree species traits, soil properties, and localized nitrogen deposition rates. It will be driven by LiDAR-derived tree canopy structure and validated against eddy covariance flux tower data (where available) and field measurements of tree growth and soil respiration from diverse urban green spaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first city-scale, mechanistic model of coupled carbon-nitrogen cycling for an urban biome, directly quantifying how nutrient constraints modulate the climate benefits of urban forests. It will provide a novel tool for urban planners to optimize green infrastructure placement and management for maximum carbon sequestration and ecosystem service co-benefits.
proposals.novelty: While C-N coupling is established in natural forest ecology, its application to the engineered and heterogeneous urban environment at hyper-resolution is novel. This moves beyond simple urban tree canopy mapping to a process-based understanding of urban forest function, integrating Malhi's expertise in tropical forest carbon dynamics with the unique biogeochemistry of cities.

proposals.title: Mapping Herbivore Functional Impact in Tropical Forests via Satellite-Derived Canopy Structure and Phenology
proposals.motivation: Large herbivores are critical ecosystem engineers, influencing forest structure, carbon cycling, and biodiversity, yet their populations are difficult to monitor directly at landscape scales. In tropical forests like the Amazon, human pressures are altering herbivore communities, with cascading effects on tree demography and ecosystem function that remain poorly quantified. Remote sensing offers a pathway to infer herbivore presence and impact indirectly through their signature on vegetation, bridging a critical gap between plot-scale ecology and regional dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial and temporal variation in satellite-derived metrics of canopy structure (e.g., texture, gap fraction) and phenology (e.g., green-up timing, leaf longevity) are correlated with, and can predict, the functional impact of herbivore communities, as validated by ground-based observations of herbivory and animal signs.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution (PlanetScope) and SAR (Sentinel-1) data with GEDI lidar waveforms to derive metrics of canopy height, density, and heterogeneity across established forest plots in the Amazon. Using machine learning (Random Forest and Convolutional Neural Networks), we will model the relationship between these structural/phenological time-series and ground-truth data on herbivore activity (from camera traps, dung counts, and leaf damage surveys). The model will be applied regionally to map probable herbivore impact zones and their correlation with anthropogenic gradients identified in Malhi's prior work on human influence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first landscape-scale, indirect monitoring framework for herbivore functional impact in tropical forests, directly linking animal ecology to forest carbon and structural science. It will quantify a key biotic control on vegetation dynamics often missing from large-scale carbon models and provide a novel tool for assessing the ecosystem consequences of defaunation.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing is used for direct animal detection or habitat mapping, applying fine-scale canopy structural metrics as a continuous proxy for herbivore *functional impact* is novel. This approach moves beyond species distribution modeling to quantify an ecosystem process, integrating Malhi's expertise in forest carbon and demography with emerging remote sensing methods.

proposals.title: Continental convergence or divergence? A pan-tropical synthesis of forest structure, dynamics, and carbon density drivers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, global understanding of tropical forest ecology and carbon dynamics is heavily skewed towards the Amazon and, to a lesser extent, Southeast Asia. African tropical forests, while a major component of the global carbon cycle and biodiversity, are underrepresented in syntheses. This limits our ability to build robust, generalizable models of forest function and resilience under global change, as continental differences in biogeography, climate history, and human influence may lead to divergent ecological trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while fundamental allometric and demographic scaling rules are universal across the tropics, the realized forest structure, carbon density, and demographic rates (growth, mortality, recruitment) will show significant continental-scale divergence, driven by distinct biogeographic histories, soil fertility gradients, and contemporary climate regimes.
proposals.method: We will integrate standardized, long-term forest inventory plot data from the RAINFOR (Amazon), AfriTRON (Africa), and T-FORCES (Southeast Asia) networks. Using a hierarchical Bayesian modelling framework, we will develop continental- and pan-tropical models for key variables: aboveground biomass (AGB), wood density, size-frequency distributions, and demographic rates. Models will explicitly incorporate covariates for climate (CHELSA), soil properties (ISRIC), and historical human modification (ArchaeoGLOBE). We will test for continental differences in model parameters and predictive power.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively balanced, data-driven synthesis of forest structure and carbon dynamics across all three tropical continents. It will directly test the generality of ecological relationships derived from the Amazon. The resulting unified models will significantly improve continental and global-scale carbon stock and flux estimates, providing a stronger empirical basis for Earth system models and climate policy.
proposals.novelty: While pan-tropical comparisons exist, they often lack the depth of standardized demographic data from Africa. This proposal's novelty lies in its explicit integration of African plot networks into a unified hierarchical modelling framework to test for continental divergence in fundamental forest properties, moving beyond simple biomass maps to mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Scaling Forest Structure and Carbon Dynamics Across the African Tropics: Integrating GEDI LiDAR, Sentinel-1 Radar, and Ground-Based Plot Networks
proposals.motivation: African tropical forests are a major, yet understudied, component of the global carbon cycle and biodiversity. Pre-2026, scaling up from intensive but sparse ground plots to continental assessments remains a critical challenge, limiting our understanding of carbon stocks, sequestration rates, and forest responses to climate change and human influence across this vast region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fusion of satellite LiDAR (GEDI) and radar (Sentinel-1) data, calibrated against a harmonized network of African forest plots, can generate robust, high-resolution maps of forest structure (canopy height, vertical profile) and aboveground carbon density, revealing continent-wide patterns and drivers of forest change.
proposals.method: We will integrate plot-level tree size, species, and carbon data from networks like AfriTRON with Level-2A GEDI waveform metrics (e.g., RH98, canopy cover) and Sentinel-1 backscatter (VV, VH). A machine learning framework (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting regression) will be trained to predict plot-level attributes from the remote sensing covariates. This model will then be applied to wall-to-wall Sentinel-1 data and GEDI footprints to generate 1-km resolution maps of forest structure and carbon stocks for the African tropics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-tropical African maps of forest structure explicitly calibrated to a continent-wide plot network. It will quantify the carbon benefits of African forests with unprecedented spatial detail and identify regions of anomalous tree size or carbon density, linking these patterns to gradients of climate, soil, and historical human activity.
proposals.novelty: While similar methods have been applied in the Amazon, the novelty lies in the tailored application to Africa's distinct forest types and environmental gradients, and the explicit integration of radar to address persistent cloud cover and complement GEDI's sampling, creating a more robust and continuous product.

proposals.title: Inferring the Dominant Drivers of Aboveground Carbon Dynamics in Amazonian Forests from Spatial Patterns of Biomass Change
proposals.motivation: Amazonian forests are experiencing complex, spatially heterogeneous changes in aboveground carbon stocks, driven by a mix of climate change, atmospheric CO2 fertilization, forest degradation, and recovery from historical disturbance. Disentangling the relative influence of these drivers is critical for predicting future carbon sequestration and informing conservation policy, yet current models struggle to attribute observed patterns to specific causes. This inverse approach directly addresses this attribution gap by using the observed spatial 'fingerprint' of carbon change to infer the underlying drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial heterogeneity in aboveground carbon change across Amazonia is not random but forms a predictable pattern that can be statistically decomposed to reveal the relative contributions of key environmental drivers, with CO2 fertilization effects dominating in core intact forests and disturbance/recovery dynamics prevailing in the human-modified arc of deforestation.
proposals.method: We will compile and harmonize pre-2026 spatially explicit datasets on aboveground carbon stocks and changes (e.g., from GEDI, airborne LiDAR, and forest inventory networks), climate, soil, and human pressure. Using a Bayesian hierarchical modelling framework, we will treat the observed spatial pattern of carbon change as the dependent variable and the driver maps as potential predictors. The model will employ variable selection techniques (e.g., spike-and-slab priors) to identify the most parsimonious set of drivers and quantify their relative weights and spatial domains of influence across the basin.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a spatially explicit, empirically grounded attribution map for carbon dynamics in Amazonia, moving beyond correlation to probabilistic inference of causation. It will directly inform Earth System Models by identifying which observed changes are consistent with CO2 fertilization versus climate stress or legacy human impacts, thereby refining projections of the Amazon's future carbon balance.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate drivers with carbon stocks, this proposal inverts the problem, using the emergent spatial pattern as the primary data to statistically back-calculate dominant driver combinations, a novel application of inverse theory in forest macroecology. It uniquely integrates Malhi's work on large-scale forest dynamics and increasing tree size with advanced spatial Bayesian methods.

ideas.title: Constraining Synthetic Community Metabolism via Atmospheric Inversion and Ecological Network Theory
ideas.description: This research fuses atmospheric inverse modeling with ecological network theory to pioneer the design and real-time diagnostics of synthetic plant communities for biotechnology. The core idea is to treat a designed community as an ecological network, where the theoretical interactions (e.g., competition, facilitation) predict its Net Primary Productivity (NPP). An atmospheric inversion model, constrained by this predicted NPP and precise measurements of local CO2 concentrations around the bioreactor or controlled environment, is then used to solve for the community's total ecosystem respiration—a holistic metabolic output. This creates a closed-loop system where deviations between predicted and observed atmospheric signals provide direct feedback on the stability and efficiency of the synthetic network, enabling iterative optimization of community composition for desired metabolic functions like carbon sequestration or compound production.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Assimilation of Remote Sensing for Predictive Stand Physiology
ideas.description: This research integrates aerospace remote sensing with a ground-based network of process-based physiological models to move beyond correlation. A hierarchical Bayesian data assimilation framework fuses multi-scale observations, including tower-based solar-induced fluorescence and leaf gas exchange, with canopy hyperspectral and LiDAR data. This approach constrains and updates mechanistic models of photosynthesis and stand structure, enabling the dynamic, validated prediction of whole-stand photosynthetic function. The system is designed to be generalizable, providing robust forecasts of forest productivity under novel environmental stressors by simulating the underlying biophysical processes.

ideas.title: Upscaling from Representative Sites: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework for PFT-Specific Phenology
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the aggregation problem by building PFT phenology models from the ground up. It begins by using multi-source geospatial data to create a global mask of representative, homogeneous pixels for each PFT, filtering out mixed, disturbed, or extreme environmental conditions. Local phenological parameters are then estimated from GPP time series at these representative sites. A hierarchical Bayesian model integrates these local estimates, explicitly partitioning variance into a global PFT-level phenological curve and local environmental effects, yielding a robust probabilistic model that captures the intrinsic phenology of the vegetation type while quantifying its context-dependent variability.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Modeling Downscaling to Bridge Molecular and Field Scales in Plant Trials
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'downscaling' approach from climate science to systematically test field-level consequences of genetic modifications in photosynthesis. In climate modeling, downscaling translates coarse global projections into high-resolution regional forecasts. The adapted solution is to create a multi-tiered predictive framework: high-throughput molecular phenotyping and controlled-environment data serve as the foundational 'global model.' This data parameterizes a mechanistic, process-based crop growth model, which acts as the downscaling engine to simulate plant performance at the field scale under a wide range of environmental conditions (soil, weather, management). This predictive bridge allows for the prioritization of the most promising genetic perturbations for costly field trials and generates hypotheses about their performance in specific agro-ecosystems, scaling molecular insights to the field level.

ideas.title: Enhance validation with diverse global field sites

ideas.title: A Data Assimilation Framework to Correct MODIS FPAR Errors and Produce Improved GPP/NPP Estimates
ideas.description: This research moves beyond diagnosing errors to actively correcting them. We develop a data assimilation system that ingests multi-source satellite data (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS, Sentinel-2) and uses a network of ground-based measurements to characterize and model systematic FPAR biases. This correction model is then integrated into a terrestrial biosphere model, where it dynamically adjusts the FPAR driver. The model is optimized against observed GPP and NPP, resulting in a novel, robust global product that quantifiably reduces uncertainty in carbon flux estimates.

ideas.title: Integrate MODIS NPP data with land management models

ideas.title: Engineering Photosynthetic Resilience for Novel and Extreme Environments
ideas.description: This research direction combines biotechnological trait modification with experimental botany in novel environments to engineer plant canopies for enhanced carbon capture under future climate conditions. It involves using controlled extreme environments—such as high-temperature, altered atmospheric composition, or erratic water availability—as experimental platforms to identify and validate key traits that optimize whole-canopy photosynthesis under stress. The core synergy is a closed-loop cycle: extreme environment experiments reveal critical functional targets, which are then engineered into plants using biotechnology, and the performance of these modified stands is validated again under the same novel conditions. This moves beyond studying adaptation or improving photosynthesis in isolation, aiming instead to design and deploy vegetation with built-in resilience for specific, anticipated environmental extremes.

ideas.title: Scale up: Global biome tipping point early warning

ideas.sourceTitle: Climate change and ecosystems: threats, opportunities and solutions

ideas.title: Data-Assimilated Inverse Design of Plant Ideotypes for Target Ecosystem Productivity
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the inverse design of plants for a target yield with data assimilation techniques from numerical weather prediction. The core idea is to use a variational data assimilation framework to constrain and refine a process-based crop or ecosystem model (like the MODIS GPP algorithm) with flux tower and remote sensing data, thereby creating a dynamically calibrated 'digital twin' of the production system. This calibrated model is then inverted to solve for the optimal set of leaf-level and molecular traits (e.g., Vcmax, SLA, rooting depth) required to achieve a specified, spatially explicit productivity target under observed historical environmental conditions. The synergy enables a move from static, theoretical ideotypes to dynamic, observationally informed designs that are robust to real-world environmental noise and can be iteratively updated as new data is assimilated.

ideas.title: Molecular-Physiological Grounding for Satellite FPAR Error Correction in GPP Models
ideas.description: This research direction integrates molecular biology and whole-plant physiology to fundamentally invert and correct the assumption of optimal MODIS FPAR. It will use biotechnological approaches (e.g., manipulating photosynthetic proteins, pigment composition, or canopy architecture in model plants) to create a controlled physiological gradient. Concurrent whole-plant and stand-level gas exchange measurements will quantify the true absorbed photosynthetically active radiation (APAR) and its efficiency. By comparing this ground-truth APAR with coincident satellite FPAR retrievals across the manipulated trait gradient, the research will establish a mechanistic, trait-based framework to predict and correct for FPAR saturation errors, thereby directly improving the physiological fidelity of GPP and NPP calculations from space.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Prediction of Plant Functional Thresholds Across Multivariate Environmental Gradients
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond cataloguing plant responses to instead predict and explain them. It integrates ecological theory and process-based models to generate explicit, testable hypotheses about how specific functional traits (e.g., hydraulic safety margins, photosynthetic thermal stability) will respond to interacting environmental drivers. Experiments are then structured along defined gradients of, for example, temperature and water deficit, to identify critical failure points and the underlying physiological mechanisms. This approach transforms observational data into a generalizable framework for forecasting plant function in novel environments.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Data Assimilation System for Adaptive Land Management Using MODIS GPP/NPP
ideas.description: This research fuses data assimilation techniques with land management applications by developing a system that continuously ingests flux tower data and MODIS GPP/NPP products into a process-based crop or forest growth model using an Ensemble Kalman Filter. The assimilated model produces dynamically updated, high-confidence estimates of current and near-future Net Primary Production (NPP) at management-relevant scales. This enables adaptive decision-support tools, such as precision fertilization schedules or harvest timing recommendations, that are automatically adjusted based on the assimilated observed conditions, improving resilience to climatic variability.

ideas.title: A Physically-Regularized Transfer Learning Framework for Generalizable Satellite-Derived NPP Bias Correction
ideas.description: This approach trains a model to predict the systematic error in MODIS NPP not as a simple residual map, but as a function of the satellite's own input variables (e.g., FPAR, meteorology) and key geospatial drivers like topography and land cover heterogeneity. To prevent overfitting to sparse validation sites, the framework employs spatial block cross-validation and a loss term that penalizes learning spurious local spatial autocorrelation. The final model is evaluated on its ability to correct bias in held-out biomes and its consistency with independent, process-based model simulations of environmental sensitivity, ensuring the corrections are generalizable and mechanistically plausible beyond the validation points.

ideas.title: Decompose GPP signal into functional type phenology using spectral unmixing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of spectral unmixing from remote sensing spectroscopy to the problem of scaling down phenology. In spectroscopy, a mixed pixel's reflectance spectrum is decomposed into pure constituent 'endmember' spectra and their abundances. Analogously, a coarse-resolution GPP time series (e.g., from a satellite pixel) is treated as a mixed temporal signal. The goal is to unmix it into its constituent 'phenological endmembers'—the characteristic GPP phenology curves for specific Plant Functional Types (like evergreen needleleaf, deciduous broadleaf, C4 grass)—and their fractional cover within the pixel. By applying and refining linear unmixing techniques against a global GPP time series and known PFT distributions, we can derive and refine generalized phenological models for specific vegetation types, effectively scaling down from the aggregate signal to its functional components.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Prescription System for Land Management Using MODIS NPP as a Vital Sign
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from precision medicine to the domain of land management. The analogous field is Precision Medicine and Clinical Decision Support, where continuous patient monitoring data is integrated with clinical models to create dynamic, personalized treatment plans. The analogous solution involves using real-time vital signs and historical patient data, analyzed against population baselines, to inform adaptive clinical interventions. Adapted to this context, the idea is to create a dynamic prescription system for land management. MODIS-derived Net Primary Productivity (NPP) data serves as a continuous 'vital sign' for a land parcel's photosynthetic activity and carbon uptake. By integrating this real-time and historical NPP data stream with agricultural or forestry management models, a decision-support framework can be developed. This system would analyze parcel-specific NPP trends against regional productivity baselines and the parcel's own history, enabling it to generate and update tailored management prescriptions. These could include recommendations for adjusting irrigation schedules, fertilizer application rates, grazing intensity, or optimal harvest windows, thereby moving from static, calendar-based management to a responsive, data-driven approach that optimizes for yield, sustainability, and climate resilience.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Maximize adaptation for fixed mitigation

ideas.sourceTitle: Climate change and ecosystems: threats, opportunities and solutions

ideas.title: A probabilistic framework for global water use efficiency mapping with integrated meteorological driver uncertainty
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the mapping of ecosystem water use efficiency (WUE = GPP/ET) with the formal propagation of input meteorological data uncertainty. It develops a Monte Carlo or analytical error propagation framework where the uncertainties in MODIS evapotranspiration and the input meteorological drivers (e.g., from reanalysis products) for both GPP and ET models are jointly characterized. This produces probabilistic WUE maps with per-pixel confidence intervals, rather than single-point estimates. The novel output allows for more reliable cross-biome comparisons, trend detection, and attribution of WUE changes by distinguishing signal from noise introduced by heterogeneous data quality.

ideas.title: Apply ecological network stability analysis to plant metabolic networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts computational and modeling tools from theoretical ecology, specifically ecological network stability analysis, to plant metabolic networks. In ecology, tools like community matrix analysis and loop analysis are used to model food webs, predicting how perturbations affect network stability and function. By treating plant metabolic pathways as analogous to species in an ecological network, these methods can be applied to model the plant metabolome. This allows researchers to predict the systemic consequences of genetic modifications or environmental stresses on metabolic output and network robustness, offering a powerful framework for metabolic engineering and understanding plant stress responses that goes beyond traditional microbial systems biology approaches.

ideas.title: Adapting Material-Specific Friction Coefficients to Biome-Specific Light Use Efficiency
ideas.description: This idea challenges the constant light use efficiency (LUE) parameter in GPP models by drawing an analogy from classical mechanics, where the assumption of a universal coefficient of friction was successfully overturned. In that field, solutions involved defining material-specific static and kinetic friction coefficients and modeling dynamic friction that varies with sliding velocity and contact conditions. Adapting this solution, the research will test if biome-specific or dynamically varying LUE parameters, conditioned on key environmental stressors, significantly improve GPP estimates. By treating LUE not as a universal constant but as a biome-specific 'efficiency coefficient' that changes with drivers like vapor pressure deficit and temperature—analogous to how friction changes with material and velocity—we can develop a more physically realistic and empirically grounded model for global carbon uptake.

ideas.title: Scale down: Microbial community response to micro-climates
ideas.description: Study soil microbial network resilience to manipulated micro-climate variability as a model for larger systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Climate change and ecosystems: threats, opportunities and solutions

ideas.title: Robustifying Land Management Decisions with Error-Characterized MODIS NPP
ideas.description: This research fuses the application of MODIS NPP for land management with the critical analysis of its foundational FPAR errors. It develops a methodology to propagate known FPAR uncertainties (e.g., saturation in dense canopies) through the NPP calculation chain to produce confidence intervals or probabilistic maps of productivity. These error-characterized NPP products are then integrated with agricultural and forestry decision models to evaluate how uncertainty impacts specific management outcomes, such as yield forecasts or harvest schedules. The result is a decision-support tool that identifies not only optimal practices based on mean NPP but also highlights scenarios where satellite data limitations pose the greatest risk to management success, enabling more robust and defensible planning.

ideas.title: Model-Guided Biotechnology for Emergent Canopy Optimization
ideas.description: This research integrates 3D canopy modeling and field-based phenotyping to identify trait combinations that avoid stand-level competitive pitfalls. Using this guidance, biotechnological tools (e.g., gene editing, synthetic biology) are applied to engineer 'socially optimal' traits, such as light-responsive pigment reallocation and coordinated stomatal regulation across canopy layers. The outcome is a closed-loop system where stand-level performance predictions directly inform genetic design, ensuring photosynthetic enhancements at the individual scale robustly improve total canopy carbon capture without degrading ecosystem resource efficiency.

ideas.title: A Geostatistical-Physiological Fusion for Continuous, Tower-Validated Remote Sensing of Stand Photosynthetic Function
ideas.description: This research direction integrates geostatistical kriging with remote sensing of stand physiology to overcome the spatial mismatch between sparse tower networks and continuous satellite or airborne imagery. By using kriging to interpolate key physiological parameters (like sun-induced chlorophyll fluorescence or tower-derived gross primary productivity) from flux tower sites into continuous validation surfaces, it creates a robust statistical scaffold. This scaffold is then used to train, calibrate, and validate machine learning models that fuse multi-spectral, hyperspectral, and LiDAR remote sensing data, producing dynamically corrected, high-resolution maps of whole-stand photosynthetic function. The synergy lies in moving beyond simple point validation to a spatially explicit, statistically rigorous calibration system that quantifies and reduces uncertainty in remote sensing products across complex terrain and heterogeneous canopies.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Detection of Ecosystem Change-Points Under Spatially Heterogeneous Meteorological Forcing Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research fuses change-point detection with spatially explicit meteorological uncertainty to create a probabilistic framework for identifying abrupt ecosystem changes. It applies statistical breakpoint models to GPP/NPP anomaly time series while simultaneously propagating spatially heterogeneous errors from input climate data through the detection algorithm. The synergy produces not just a location and timing of a potential change-point, but also a confidence metric that accounts for local data quality. This allows for robust, uncertainty-aware mapping of ecosystem disturbances, filtering out spurious detections caused by unreliable meteorological inputs rather than true ecological transitions.

ideas.title: A decision-centric cost-benefit framework to quantify the spatial resolution required for actionable agricultural and conservation management
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a generic resolution critique by linking data scale directly to management outcomes. It formalizes concrete local decisions, such as delineating wetland boundaries for protection or prescribing variable-rate irrigation, and defines the spatial precision each decision demands. Using a simulation-based approach, it degrades high-resolution field or UAV-derived reference data to coarser scales (including 1-km) and measures the resulting error in the decision output and its associated cost. The result is a robust, quantitative framework that specifies for which decisions, and in what landscape contexts, 1-km resolution is insufficient, and identifies the cost-effective resolution needed for actionable management.

ideas.title: A Sentinel Network for Ecological Model Validation
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of limited validation for ecological models by drawing an analogy from clinical drug trials. In medicine, multi-center trial networks validate treatments across diverse human populations using standardized protocols. Adapted to ecology, the solution is to establish a formal 'Global Ecological Validation Network' (GEVN). This network would function as a coordinated consortium of sentinel field sites, strategically chosen to represent the planet's major biomes, disturbance regimes (e.g., wildfire frequency, insect outbreaks, land-use change), and climatic gradients. By implementing common, rigorous protocols for data collection and model testing across all network sites, and governed by a shared framework for data and analysis, this approach would systematically assess and enhance model generalizability, moving validation beyond ad-hoc, geographically limited efforts to a structured, globally representative benchmark system.

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Control Analysis to Engineer Synthetic Plant Community Networks
ideas.description: This idea imports a solution from systems biology to expand the original seed of applying ecological network theory to biotechnology. The analogous field is Metabolic Engineering, specifically the use of Metabolic Control Analysis (MCA) to understand and engineer biochemical pathways. MCA's solution is a quantitative framework that maps control distribution in a network to identify key leverage points. Adapted to synthetic plant communities, this involves developing an 'Ecological Control Analysis' framework. This framework would model plant species as network nodes and their interactions (e.g., competition, facilitation, allelopathy) as fluxes. By calculating control coefficients for community functions, it would predict which species or interactions are most critical to engineer—such as introducing a genetically modified nitrogen-fixer to replace a high-control competitor—thereby allowing for the stable, optimized design of synthetic ecosystems for agriculture, bioremediation, or bioproduction.

ideas.title: A stable anomaly baseline with quantified sensitivity analysis for climate trend detection
ideas.description: This research establishes a fixed, long-term reference period for calculating MODIS-based climate anomalies to ensure a temporally consistent data record for trend analysis. In parallel, it systematically runs a moving window analysis to quantify how key metrics (e.g., trend magnitude, detection year of a significant shift) depend on the choice of baseline period. The sensitivity results are published as core metadata, explicitly characterizing the structural uncertainty introduced by baseline definition. This approach provides a stable primary product for users while rigorously diagnosing and documenting the robustness of observed changes.

ideas.title: Environmentally Robust Inverse Design of Plant Ideotypes via Multi-Scale Simulation and Evolutionary Optimization
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem to design not for a single target yield but for a yield probability distribution across a target environmental landscape. It employs a closed-loop system where a forward multi-scale simulator (integrating gene networks, leaf physiology, and canopy dynamics) is coupled with an evolutionary optimization algorithm. The algorithm inversely searches the high-dimensional trait space for ideotypes whose simulated stand performance is both high-yielding and stable across thousands of stochastic environmental scenarios. The final output is a set of Pareto-optimal trait combinations, each mapped to genomic intervention strategies, that are robust to real-world GxE interactions and community-level emergence.

ideas.title: A Closed-Loop Multi-Scale Phenotyping Pipeline to Mechanistically Link Targeted Perturbations to Emergent Photosynthetic Performance
ideas.description: This research program establishes a causal, predictive bridge from molecular biology to crop-scale performance by enforcing a closed-loop design. It begins with precise molecular perturbations in key photosynthetic pathways and subjects these plants to dynamic, environmentally realistic conditions in automated phenotyping platforms, capturing detailed physiological responses. Concurrent multi-omics profiling from the same plants provides the molecular state data, which is integrated with physiological data into a mechanistic model to generate testable predictions for stand-level photosynthetic efficiency. These predictions are then rigorously validated in replicated field trials, creating a feedback loop that refines both the molecular targets and the cross-scale models.

ideas.title: Adapting Microbial Extremophile Cultivation Methods for Novel Plant Stress Chambers
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from microbiology and astrobiology, where specialized bioreactors are used to cultivate extremophiles by replicating complex extreme environments, this idea proposes adapting that solution for experimental botany. The research would involve designing and deploying Novel Environment Plant Chambers (NEPCs)—closed-loop, multi-parameter growth systems. These chambers would go beyond standard climate controls to dynamically and precisely combine novel factors like exotic atmospheric gas ratios, non-Earth pressure regimes, synthetic light spectra, and unique hydrologic/soil conditions. This platform would enable the first systematic studies of plant physiology, development, and resilience under entirely novel, stable, and reproducible environmental scenarios, expanding experimental botany into realms previously limited to theoretical speculation or microbial models.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Computational Framework for Plant Systems Biology Informed by Adapted Microbial Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This research develops a new computational framework for plant metabolic networks by first critically assessing and adapting tools from microbial systems biology. It explicitly incorporates plant-specific biological constraints—including multicellular tissue organization, subcellular compartmentalization, and the unique role of secondary metabolism—into the core model architecture. The approach creates hybrid models that combine transferable microbial concepts (like constraint-based reconstruction) with novel modules for apoplastic/symplastic transport and developmental stage transitions. This enables accurate, predictive modeling of plant metabolic processes that are fundamentally different from microbial systems.

ideas.title: A Geostatistical Data Fusion Framework for High-Resolution, Locally-Calibrated NPP Mapping
ideas.description: This research synthesizes high-resolution downscaling with geostatistical validation to create a novel, self-calibrating mapping framework. It uses kriging on sparse eddy covariance tower NPP measurements to generate continuous, probabilistic validation surfaces that quantify spatial uncertainty. These surfaces then directly guide the data fusion of MODIS and Landsat imagery, tuning the downscaling model parameters locally to minimize bias against the interpolated ground truth. The result is a robust, 30-meter NPP product that leverages spatial statistics to ensure regional consistency with tower measurements while capturing fine-scale heterogeneity from Landsat.

ideas.title: Constraint-Satisfaction Mapping from Ecosystem Topology to Permissible Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research program formalizes the inversion of the scaling paradigm by treating ecosystem-level constraints (like stoichiometric balances and network resilience) as boundary conditions for a mechanistic search space. It employs computational models to generate all biophysically plausible molecular-to-organismal pathways that could operate within these boundaries. The approach is validated through targeted experiments designed to falsify specific predicted pathway necessities, ensuring hypotheses are causal and testable, not merely correlative.

ideas.title: Apply Ant Colony Optimization Principles to Engineer Canopy Light-Use Efficiency
ideas.description: This idea applies the problem-solving framework from Swarm Intelligence, specifically Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), to the challenge of enhancing stand-level photosynthesis. In ACO, decentralized agents (ants) use simple local rules and stigmergic communication (pheromones) to solve complex global optimization problems like finding the shortest path. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing a tree canopy as a decentralized system. The research would employ biotechnological tools to modify traits in individual leaves—such as phototropic response mechanisms and intercellular signaling pathways—so they can sense and react to local light conditions and signals from neighbors. The goal is to engineer a self-organizing canopy where these decentralized adjustments collectively lead to emergent, stand-level optimization of light distribution and carbon capture, significantly improving upon static, genetically uniform architectures.

ideas.title: Spatially Explicit, Meteorologically-Informed Error Propagation for Continuous NPP Validation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the propagation of non-uniform meteorological data uncertainty with geostatistical interpolation of tower measurements. It develops a co-kriging or stochastic simulation approach where the spatial covariance structure of the meteorological driver errors is jointly modeled with the spatial structure of the NPP residuals from the tower network. This method produces not just a continuous map of predicted NPP error (the validation surface), but also a continuous map of the confidence in that error estimate, quantifying how uncertainty from imperfect meteorological inputs propagates and interacts spatially with the sparsity of the validation network. The result is a comprehensive, spatially explicit error budget for regional/global NPP products that distinguishes error sources and guides targeted improvements in both model inputs and validation infrastructure.

ideas.title: Apply Ensemble Kalman Filter Data Assimilation from Oceanography to MODIS GPP Model
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) data assimilation method, a cornerstone of modern ocean state estimation, to improve satellite-derived Gross Primary Production (GPP) models. In oceanography, EnKF is used to assimilate sparse in-situ data from floats and moorings into complex circulation models, updating an ensemble of states to correct variables and constrain parameters, yielding improved reanalyses. Adapted here, an ensemble of the MODIS GPP model would be run, with members perturbed across key uncertain parameters and inputs. Flux tower GPP measurements would then be assimilated via the EnKF to sequentially update the ensemble across the spatial domain. This produces a dynamically consistent, high-resolution GPP field that optimally fuses the global coverage of MODIS with the local accuracy of flux towers, while providing formal estimates of posterior uncertainty, analogous to an ocean reanalysis but for the terrestrial carbon cycle.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Kriging System for Real-Time GPP Anomaly Correction and Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This research develops an operational framework that fuses the generation of low-latency GPP anomaly maps with a dynamic geostatistical kriging system. The kriging model uses incoming data from the sparse flux tower network not just to create validation surfaces, but to actively correct and constrain the satellite-driven GPP anomaly estimates in near-real-time. This synergy produces a continuously improving product where the spatial statistics inform the anomaly detection algorithm's confidence and bias, and the rapid anomaly maps provide context to optimize the kriging interpolation parameters dynamically. The result is a validated, uncertainty-quantified GPP anomaly product with significantly improved accuracy and reliability for immediate use in drought or carbon flux monitoring.

ideas.title: Adapt a Nowcasting Pipeline from Meteorology for Real-Time GPP Anomaly Detection
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the latency problem in producing Gross Primary Production (GPP) anomaly maps by drawing an analogy to operational weather nowcasting. In meteorology, real-time anomaly products (like precipitation nowcasts) are generated through streamlined, automated pipelines that assimilate rapid-update observations into simplified models. The adapted research idea is to develop a similar nowcasting pipeline for terrestrial carbon uptake. This would involve creating an automated system that integrates near-real-time satellite data (e.g., solar-induced fluorescence from geostationary sensors, reflectance indices) and instantaneous meteorological drivers into a computationally efficient model or machine learning framework. The system would continuously generate and disseminate GPP anomaly maps at high temporal frequency (e.g., daily) with a latency of less than two days, enabling operational monitoring of vegetation stress, drought impacts, and sudden disturbance events for applications in agriculture, forestry, and climate science.

ideas.title: Observation-constrained inference of non-stationary meteorological error fields for robust NPP uncertainty quantification
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple propagation of assumed input errors by developing a data-driven method to characterize the often unknown and non-stationary error structure of meteorological drivers. It uses a hierarchical Bayesian framework that assimilates high-quality, localized observations (e.g., flux tower data, targeted remote sensing) to infer spatio-temporal error covariances and potential biases in the gridded meteorological inputs. These inferred, realistic error fields are then sampled to create perturbed driver ensembles, which are fed into an ensemble of NPP model formulations. The final output provides a robust, observationally anchored uncertainty estimate for NPP that accounts for the compounded effects of poorly characterized input data errors and model structural sensitivity.

ideas.title: Adaptive Baseline Periods Using a Rolling Reference Frame
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of a rolling calculation window from financial time series analysis to the problem of updating climatological baselines. In finance, moving averages continuously update by incorporating new data and dropping old data to maintain a relevant trend baseline. Adapted to MODIS climatology, this means implementing a rolling 30-year baseline period that advances forward one year with each new annual data release. For example, the baseline would shift from 1991-2020 to 1992-2021 once 2021 data is available. This solution automatically extends the baseline as the data record lengthens, ensuring anomaly calculations are always relative to the most recent three-decade climate normal. It provides a systematic, rule-based approach for operational data products that inherently accounts for a shifting climate mean without abrupt transitions between fixed reference periods.

ideas.description: This system generates operational GPP anomaly maps by applying advanced data assimilation to an ensemble of models and satellite data streams, prioritizing robustness over minimal latency. It produces not just an anomaly value, but also layers quantifying confidence, inter-model agreement, and probable attribution to environmental drivers or data issues. This design ensures the product remains useful and interpretable even during partial data outages or ambiguous signals, directly serving decision-support systems that require reliable, actionable intelligence on ecosystem stress.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Process-ML Framework for Spatiotemporally Dynamic Light Use Efficiency and Bias-Aware GPP Estimation
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid framework that embeds a machine-learned, dynamic Light Use Efficiency (LUE) module within a process-based GPP model (e.g., MODIS GPP algorithm). Instead of applying ML for a one-time, black-box correction of final NPP estimates, the system trains ML models (e.g., gradient boosting or neural networks) to predict the spatiotemporal residual errors of the process model as a function of environmental and physiological drivers. These learned error patterns are then used to dynamically adjust the LUE parameter in near-real-time, effectively making the core process variable biome-aware, condition-specific, and self-correcting. This yields a transparent, updatable model that retains physical structure while achieving the accuracy of pure ML, fundamentally advancing beyond static parameterization or empirical bias correction alone.
